Tree Phenology Wi/Se 2025/26 1 The start of the module

Tree Phenology Wi/Se 2025/26 Karl Kleiß 2026-03-10 1 The start of the module This website documents my learning progress in the module Tree Phenology. At the beginning of the semester, I had very limited experience with RMarkdown, so I first had to familiarize myself with its structure, syntax, and typical workflows. To ensure a smoother start into the course, I began working with RMarkdown even before the official start of the module. Over time, this allowed me to gradually improve my technical skills, develop a clearer understanding of how to create reproducible reports, and apply these tools more confidently to scientific questions related to tree phenology. In addition to acquiring technical skills, an important focus of the module is the concept of tree dormancy. Tree dormancy refers to a physiological state in which growth processes are temporarily suspended in response to environmental conditions, especially during the colder season. This state is not a passive pause, but a highly regulated process that helps perennial plants, such as apple trees, survive unfavorable conditions like low temperatures and reduced light availability. Dormancy plays a crucial role in synchronizing growth and developmental stages with seasonal changes. For example, in apple trees, a properly fulfilled dormancy period supports uniform bud break and flowering in spring, which can ultimately contribute to improved fruit set and higher yields. Understanding these mechanisms is essential both for basic research in phenology and for practical applications in horticulture and agriculture.

2 Chapter 3 - Tree Dormancy 2.1 Questions 2.2 Answers

2 Chapter 3 - Tree Dormancy 2.1 Questions Put yourself in the place of a breeder who wants to calculate the temperature requirements of a newly released cultivar. Which method will you use to calculate the chilling and forcing periods? Please justify your answer. Which are the advantages (2) of the BBCH scale compared with earlies scales? Classify the phenological stages of sweet cherry according to the BBCH scale. 2.2 Answers 1. Calculating chilling and forcing As a breeder, I would use the Dynamic Model (Chill Portions Model) to calculate the chilling and forcing requirements of a new sweet cherry cultivar. This model best represents the dynamic transitions between endodormancy and ecodormancy shown in the figure, including temperature-dependent processes such as deacclimation, transport restoration, and hormonal regulation. It accounts for the accumulation and partial reversal of chill portions under fluctuating temperatures, making it more biologically realistic and reliable under variable climate conditions. 2. Advantages of the BBCH Compared to the older scales developed by Fleckinger (1948) and Baggiolini (1952), the BBCH scale offers higher precision and international comparability. The BBCH system uses numerical codes ranging from 00 to 99 instead of letters or descriptive terms, which allows researchers to measure and reproduce small differences between cultivars or environmental conditions in a more objective way. While the Fleckinger and Baggiolini scales were mainly regional and species-specific, the BBCH scale provides a standardized framework that can be applied across different plant species, regions, and research institutions. This makes it especially valuable for plant breeding, climate change studies, and the modelling of dormancy processes. The BBCH system enables consistent comparisons and integration into modern scientific and digital systems. 3. 3 Chapter 4 - Climate Change 3.1 Questions 3.2 Answers

3 Chapter 4 - Climate Change 3.1 Questions List the main drivers of climate change at the decade to century scale, and briefly explain the mechanism through which the currently most important driver affects our climate. Explain briefly what is special about temperature dynamics of recent decades, and why we have good reasons to be concerned. What does the abbreviation ‘RCP’ stand for, how are RCPs defined, and what is their role in projecting future climates? 3.2 Answers 1. Main drivers of climate change and key mechanism Figure 3.1: Greenhouse Effect Increases as Humans Emit More Greenhouse Gases (GHGs): (Education 2026). Climate change is influenced by several factors. The main drivers at the decade-to-century scale are greenhouse gases, aerosols, changes in surface albedo, clouds and ozone, and small variations in solar activity or volcanic eruptions. Today, the most important driver is the increase of greenhouse gases caused by humans, mainly through burning fossil fuels. Greenhouse gases let short-wave sunlight reach the Earth’s surface but absorb and re-emit long-wave heat radiation from the surface. This process traps heat and keeps the planet about 30 °C warmer than it would otherwise be. The recent rise in greenhouse gases therefore leads to additional warming of the climate system. 2. Temperature dynamics of recent decades Figure 3.2: Increase in global surface temperatures between 1850 and 2023 compared to 1850–1900: (Efbrazil 2026). Global temperatures have increased faster than at any other time in human history. The ten warmest years on record all occurred within the last two decades. This change cannot be explained by natural factors alone but is clearly linked to human greenhouse gas emissions. Compared with the past 2,000 years, the current warming is unusually quick and strong. Such rapid change creates serious risks, because natural and human systems cannot easily adapt.It also increases the chance of positive feedbacks, such as melting ice and permafrost, which can make warming even stronger. For these reasons, scientists are very concerned about the current temperature trends. 3. Meaning and role of RCPs Figure 3.3: Different RCP scenarios result in different predicted greenhouse gas concentrations in the atmosphere (from 2000 to 2100). RCP8.5 would result in the highest greenhouse gas concentration (measured as CO2-equivalents): (EfbraziI 2026). ‘RCP’ stands for Representative Concentration Pathway. RCPs are greenhouse-gas concentration scenarios used by the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) to project future climates. Each RCP is defined by the radiative forcing level expected by the year 2100 for example, RCP 2.6 W/m² (low emissions) or RCP 8.5 W/m² (high emissions). Climate models use these RCPs to calculate possible future temperatures, rainfall, and other climate variables. By comparing different RCPs, scientists can estimate how much future climate change depends on human emission choices. References Education, CFR. 2026. “The Greenhouse Effect.” https://earthobservatory.nasa.gov/features/GreenhouseEffect. EfbraziI. 2026. “IPCC Representative Concentration Pathways.” https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Representative_Concentration_Pathway. Efbrazil. 2026. “Global Surface Temperature.” https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Globale_Erw%C3%A4rmung#/media/Datei:Global_Temperature_And_Forces.svg.

4 Chapter 5 - Winter chill projections 4.1 Questions: Sketch out three data access and processing challenges that had to be overcome in order to produce chill projections with state-of-the-art methodology. Outline, in your understanding, the basic steps that are necessary to make such projections. 4.2 Answers: The three main challenges are in the preperation of the avaible data. One of the bigggest problems is the lack of data itself in one region. Sometimes there are weather stations, but with big time gaps. Therefore you have to search for other weather stations nearby to complete the weather data, to get rid of the time gap. Figure 4.1: Spatial distribution of weather stations in the Global Historical Climatology Network in 2020: (Ortiz-Bobea 2021). Looking for other weather stations nearby you also have to be cautious. These weather stations can be in different heights, and therefore provide very different data. This is especially bad for modelling. If you assume that these two weather stations are nearby to each other and should provide the same data, this will lead to bad modeling. The same height problem applies for rasta-data for future climate modeling. Figure 4.2: Change in average atmospheric temperature with altitude in the standard atmosphere. This graph also identifies the various named layers and transition zones found in the atmosphere: (Pidwirny 2021). Another problem is the nature of the data. Many weather stations record their data on a daily basis(Tmin/Tmax/Tmean). One reason for this is that it means significantly less data has to be stored. However, this data then has to be modeled back to hourly data. The reason for this is as follows: Chill models primarily work with hourly weather data. Figure 4.3: Half-hourly temperature data (clear circles) estimated from AWAP daily: (Zhang et al. 2015) As already discussed, the data must also be modeled. Modeling usually requires high computing power, as a lot of data has to be evaluated. The raw data can easily run into double-digit gigabytes. This data can no longer be evaluated by humans. Therefore, automation must take place! Considering all the problems we stated in exercise 1, we can now try to process the data. I made a flow chart to give a better overview, instead of plain text. 6 Chapter 7 - Chill Models 6.1 Questions 6.2 Answers 6.3 Plot

6 Chapter 7 - Chill Models In this chapter, winter chill accumulation was analysed using the chillR package. Winter chill is an essential requirement for many temperate fruit trees, as sufficient cold exposure during dormancy is necessary for proper bud break and flowering in spring. First, the chilling() function was applied to the Winters_hours_gaps dataset to calculate chill accumulation within a defined time period. This step demonstrates how hourly temperature data can be transformed into biologically meaningful chill units. Next, a custom temperature-weighting model was developed using the step_model() function. By defining specific temperature ranges and assigning different weights to them, the model reflects the assumption that chill effectiveness varies across temperatures. Moderate temperatures contribute most to chill accumulation, whereas very low or high temperatures have little or no effect. Finally, the custom model was applied and compared with an established model (GDH) using tempResponse(). This highlights how different modelling approaches can lead to different estimates of chill accumulation, depending on their underlying temperature response assumptions. 6.1 Questions Run the chilling() function on the Winters_hours_gap dataset Create your own temperature-weighting chill model using the step_model() function Run this model on the Winters_hours_gaps dataset using the tempResponse() function. 6.2 Answers 1. Running the chilling() function. library(chillR) out1 <- chilling( make_JDay(Winters_hours_gaps), Start_JDay = 90, End_JDay = 100 ) out1 2. Little changes, but its mine! df <- data.frame( lower = c(-1000, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6), upper = c( 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 1000), weight = c( 0, 1, 2, 3, 2, 1, 0) ) my_chill_model <- function(x) step_model(x, df = df) 3. Applying the tempResponse() function to the data set. out3 <- tempResponse( make_JDay(Winters_hours_gaps), Start_JDay = 90, End_JDay = 100, models = list( My_Chill = my_chill_model, GDH = GDH ) ) out3 6.3 Plot Plot for visualization of the processed data: Figure 6.1: Temperature response of custom chill model between 31st March and 10th of April. Code for the plot: # Temperature temp <- seq(-5, 15, by = 0.1) # Weight weights <- my_chill_model(temp) # Plot plot( temp, weights, type = "s", xlab = "Temperature (°C)", ylab = "Chill weight", main = "Temperature response of custom chill model" ) abline(h = 0, lty = 2, col = "grey")
klagenfurt-austria.html
7 Klagenfurt, Austria

7 Klagenfurt, Austria I have visited Klagenfurt approximately ten times during the summer months. The surrounding agricultural landscape is predominantly conventional, with maize cultivation playing a major role. Due to the comparatively low population density, many households own larger gardens than is typical in the Bonn region. These gardens are often used as small, non-commercial orchards. Based on informal conversations with orchard owners, these trees are generally not intensively managed. Nevertheless, they produce a reliable annual harvest that can supply several households. This suggests that the local climatic conditions are suitable for apple cultivation. The summer climate is comparable to that of the Bonn region, allowing sufficient heat accumulation for fruit development. Winters in Klagenfurt are typically colder and longer; however, temperatures generally remain within the tolerance range of common apple varieties. While severe frost events can occasionally occur, they do not usually cause widespread or lasting damage to established trees in valley locations. It should also be noted that temperature conditions change significantly with altitude. This is particularly relevant in Carinthia (Kärnten), the federal state in which Klagenfurt is located. The region is characterized by numerous valleys surrounded by alpine terrain. These valleys provide comparatively moderate microclimatic conditions and are already widely used for pasture farming and settlement, which further indicates their agricultural suitability. With regard to concerns about low temperatures in winter, the trend, as here in Bonn, is toward temperatures in the region continuing to rise. Figure 7.1: Klagenfurt temperature trends throughout the years.

9 Chapter 9 - Some useful tools in R Since there are a lot of questions in Chapter 9, I skipped the part of explanation. The chapter is very straight forward and rather about “definitions” than a personal opinion. Answers 1 - tidyverse & Pipes 1a Convert the dataset to a tibble library(tidyverse) WHG <- as_tibble(Winter_hours_gaps) 1b - Select only the first 10 rows WHG_10 <- WHG %>% slice_head(n = 10) 1c - Convert to long format (Temp & Temp_gaps) WHG_long <- WHG_10 %>% pivot_longer( cols = c(Temp_gaps, Temp), names_to = "Variable", values_to = "Value" ) 1d - ggplot2: Faceted plot ggplot(WHG_long, aes(x = Day, y = Value)) + geom_line() + facet_wrap(~Variable, scales = "free_y") + theme_minimal() 1e - Convert back to wide format WHG_wide <- WHG_long %>% pivot_wider( names_from = Variable, values_from = Value ) 1f - Select columns (Year, Month, Day, Temp) WHG_sel <- WHG_wide %>% select(Year, Month, Day, Temp) 1g - Sort by Temp (descending) WHG_sorted <- WHG_sel %>% arrange(desc(Temp)) 2 - for loop (convert Temp to Fahrenheit) Formula: °F = °C × 9/5 + 32 WHG_loop <- Winter_hours_gaps for (i in 1:nrow(WHG_loop)) { WHG_loop$Temp[i] <- WHG_loop$Temp[i] * 9/5 + 32 } 3 - apply family WHG_apply <- Winter_hours_gaps WHG_apply$Temp <- sapply(WHG_apply$Temp, function(x) x * 9/5 + 32) 4 - tidyverse + mutate WHG_mutate <- Winter_hours_gaps %>% mutate(Temp = Temp * 9/5 + 32)

Produce chill distributions for these scenarios and plot them. 13.2 Answers 1. Finding the location for extracting then computing and summarizing the data: library(chillR) library(dplyr) library(ggplot2) # Loading Temperature data from previous lessons Klagenfurt_temps <- read.csv2("data/Klagenfurt_temps.csv") scenario_1980 <- temperature_scenario_from_records(weather = Klagenfurt_temps, year = 1980) scenario_1980$'1980'$data temps_1980 <- temperature_generation(weather = Klagenfurt_temps, years = c(1973,2019), sim_years = c(2001,2100), temperature_scenario = scenario_1980) #adjust baseline scenario_1996 <- temperature_scenario_from_records(weather = Klagenfurt_temps, year = 1996) # Relative scenario relative_scenario <- temperature_scenario_baseline_adjustment( baseline = scenario_1996, temperature_scenario = scenario_1980) # apply the relative scenario temps_1980<-temperature_generation(weather = Klagenfurt_temps, years = c(1973,2019), sim_years = c(2001,2100), temperature_scenario = relative_scenario) # generating all past scenarios all_past_scenarios <- temperature_scenario_from_records( weather = Klagenfurt_temps, year = c(1980, 1990, 2000, 2010)) adjusted_scenarios <- temperature_scenario_baseline_adjustment( baseline = scenario_1996, temperature_scenario = all_past_scenarios) all_past_scenario_temps <- temperature_generation( weather = Klagenfurt_temps, years = c(1973,2019), sim_years = c(2001,2100), temperature_scenario = adjusted_scenarios) save_temperature_scenarios(all_past_scenario_temps, "data", "Klagenfurt_hist_scenarios_chapter14") # Frost model function frost_model <- function(x) step_model(x, data.frame( lower=c(-1000,0), upper=c(0,1000), weight=c(1,0))) models <- list(Chill_Portions = Dynamic_Model, GDH = GDH, Frost_H = frost_model) chill_hist_scenario_list <- tempResponse_daily_list(all_past_scenario_temps, latitude = 46.62, Start_JDay = 305, End_JDay = 59, models = models) #saving the data chill_hist_scenario_list <- lapply(chill_hist_scenario_list, function(x) x %>% filter(Perc_complete == 100)) save_temperature_scenarios(chill_hist_scenario_list, "data","Klagenfurt_hist_chill_305_59_Chapter_14") # Lets sort the data and make a nice plot out of it scenarios <- names(chill_hist_scenario_list)[1:4] all_scenarios <- chill_hist_scenario_list[[scenarios[1]]] %>% mutate(scenario = as.numeric(scenarios[1])) for (sc in scenarios[2:4]) all_scenarios <- all_scenarios %>% rbind(chill_hist_scenario_list[[sc]] %>% cbind( scenario=as.numeric(sc)) ) %>% filter(Perc_complete == 100) # Let's compute the actual 'observed' chill for comparison actual_chill <- tempResponse_daily_list(Klagenfurt_temps, latitude=46.62, Start_JDay = 305, End_JDay = 59, models)[[1]] %>% filter(Perc_complete == 100) ggplot(data = all_scenarios, aes(scenario, Chill_Portions, fill = factor(scenario))) + geom_violin() + ylab("Chill accumulation (Chill Portions)") + xlab("Scenario year") + theme_bw(base_size = 15) + ylim(c(0,90)) + geom_point(data = actual_chill, aes(End_year, Chill_Portions, fill = "blue"), col = "blue", show.legend = FALSE) + scale_fill_discrete(name = "Scenario", breaks = unique(all_scenarios$scenario)) 2. chapter-15---future-temperature-scenarios.html
14 Chapter 15 - Future temperature scenarios 14.1 Questions 14.2 Answer

14 Chapter 15 - Future temperature scenarios 14.1 Questions Briefly describe the differences between the RCPs and the SSPs (you may have to follow some of the links provided above) 14.2 Answer Representative Concentration Pathways (RCPs) and Shared Socioeconomic Pathways (SSPs) are two generations of climate change scenarios used to describe possible future climates. RCPs were used in earlier climate research and in the CMIP5 climate models. They describe different possible futures only in terms of greenhouse gas concentrations and the resulting radiative forcing in the atmosphere by the year 2100. Each RCP therefore represents a specific level of warming, depending on how much greenhouse gas is emitted. However, RCPs do not explain why emissions change, because they do not include social or economic development. Figure 14.1: Predicted atmospheric CO₂ concentrations for different shared socioeconomic pathways (SSPs) across the 21st century (projected by MAGICC7, a simple/reduced complexity climate model). Each data point represents an average of simulated values generated from five integrated assessment models: (Sfdiversity 2020). SSPs are the newer generation of scenarios and are used with CMIP6 models. In contrast to RCPs, SSPs include socioeconomic development. They describe how population growth, economic development, technology, energy use, and political decisions may evolve in the future. Therefore, SSPs combine human development pathways with climate projections and provide a more realistic description of future climate change. In summary, RCPs focus mainly on physical climate forcing (greenhouse gas concentrations), while SSPs additionally include human society and development pathways. Because of this, SSPs are now considered more up-to-date and more suitable for future climate impact studies. Figure 14.2: Conceptual framework for evaluating vulnerability and adaptation to climate change: (Luedeling 2020) References Luedeling, Eike. 2020. “The Challenge of Warming Winters: Do We Understand Tree Dormancy Enough to Prepare Deciduous Orchards in Warm Places?” Acta Horticulturae 1281: 441–48. https://doi.org/10.17660/ActaHortic.2020.1281.58. Sfdiversity. 2020. “Predicted Atmospheric CO₂ Concentrations for Different Shared Socioeconomic Pathways (SSPs) Across the 21st Century.” https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Shared_Socioeconomic_Pathways#/media/File:Atmospheric_CO%E2%82%82_concentrations_by_SSP_across_the_21st_century.svg.

19 Chapter 20 - Simple phenology analysis 19.1 Questions 19.2 Answers

19 Chapter 20 - Simple phenology analysis 19.1 Questions Provide a brief narrative describing what p-hacking is, and why this is a problematic approach to data analysis. Provide a sketch of your causal understanding of the relationship between temperature and bloom dates. What do we need to know to build a process-based model from this? 19.2 Answers 1. What is p-hacking and why is it problematic? P-hacking describes the practice of testing many different statistical models or variable combinations until a statistically significant result (p < 0.05) is found. This is problematic because significant correlations can appear purely by chance, especially in large datasets. Such relationships may not represent real biological mechanisms. As shown in the chapter, complex models (e.g., high-order polynomials) can fit the data very well but still have no biological meaning. Therefore, p-hacking can lead to false conclusions and reduces the scientific reliability of the analysis. 2. Causal understanding of the relationship between temperature and bloom dates Time (calendar year) does not directly influence tree phenology. Trees do not respond to the year itself. Instead, temperature is the causal driver. Bloom dates appear correlated with year only because temperatures have increased over time. Temperature affects physiological processes in the tree, which then determine the timing of flowering. Thus, the correct causal chain is: Year -> Temperature -> Tree physiology -> Bloom date More specifically, bloom timing is controlled by two temperature-dependent processes: winter chilling and spring heat accumulation (forcing). 3. What is required to build a process-based model? To build a process-based phenology model, we need biological and physiological information rather than only statistical relationships. Important components include: the chilling requirement (amount of winter cold needed to break dormancy) the forcing or heat requirement (amount of warmth needed for bud development) the timing of chilling and forcing periods the tree’s temperature response functions (how temperature contributes to chilling and heat accumulation) Such a model aims to represent the biological processes that cause flowering, not only correlations in the data. Bloom and Temperatures Figure 19.1: Illustration of the relation between bloom date and temperatures in Klagenfurt, Austria.

20 Chapter 21 - PLS Regression Plots 20.1 Questions Briefly explain why you shouldn’t take the results of a PLS regression analysis between temperature and phenology at face value. What do you need in addition in order to make sense of such outputs? Replicate the PLS analysis for the Roter Boskoop dataset that you used in a previous lesson. Write down your thoughts on why we’re not seeing the temperature response pattern we may have expected. What happened to the chill response? 20.2 Answers 1. PLS Regression Very few observations (one flowering date per year) Many predictors (temperature on many days) PLS is data-driven (data mining) → can also find random patterns Do not overinterpret individual peaks or sign changes Without prior biological knowledge (chilling vs. forcing), the patterns are meaningless 2. PLS Analysis visualized I did the PLS Analysis using the Roter Boskoop Dataset and the location of Klagenfurt, Austria. Figure 20.1: PLS Analysis for chill between the months October and April. Data used from 1973 until 2019. 3. Why do the results differ from expectation? Chill phase occurs later in autumn, outside the analysis window. Chilling is less temperature-sensitive than forcing. Strong correlation between days → signal becomes “smeared”. PLS prioritizes the strongest signal → this is usually spring warmth. Possible overlap of chill and heat.
chapter-22---successes-and-limitations-of-pls-regression-analysis.html
21 Chapter 22 - Successes and limitations of PLS regression analysis 21.1 Questions 21.2 Answers

21 Chapter 22 - Successes and limitations of PLS regression analysis 21.1 Questions Briefly explain in what climatic settings we can expect PLS regression to detect the chilling phase - and in what settings this probably won’t work. How could we overcome this problem? 21.2 Answers 1. Problems with PLS PLS regression can detect the chilling phase when there is a clear and consistent relationship between winter temperature and chill accumulation. This occurs in relatively warm winter climates, where higher temperatures directly reduce chilling. In such cases, temperature and phenology are monotonically related, so the statistical method can identify the effect. However, in colder climates this relationship is not consistent. When winters are cold, a moderate increase in temperature may actually increase chilling, while higher temperatures reduce it. Because the relationship between temperature and chilling is not monotonic, PLS regression cannot clearly identify the chilling phase. Therefore, the method works best in mild winter regions (e.g., California) and performs poorly in colder regions such as Germany or northern China. Figure 21.1: Seasonal progression of canopy phenology in cold-limited trees, detailing key stages such as budset, paradormancy, endodormancy and ecodormancy, and including the physiological processes during winter dormancy, including hormone regulation and the influence of chilling and forcing temperatures on budbreak and shoot elongation: (Silvestro et al. 2025). 2. Solutions for the problem The problem can be overcome by using process-based phenology models instead of relying only on statistical correlations. Rather than directly relating temperature to bloom dates, we should first calculate biologically meaningful variables, such as chill accumulation and heat accumulation, using chill and forcing models. These derived variables can then be related to phenology. By incorporating ecological knowledge about dormancy processes, the analysis becomes more robust and allows detection of the chilling phase even when the temperature relationship is not monotonic. References Silvestro, R., A. Deslauriers, P. Prislan, et al. 2025. “From Roots to Leaves: Tree Growth Phenology in Forest Ecosystems.” Current Forestry Reports. https://doi.org/10.1007/s40725-025-00245-9.

chapter-24---examples-of-pls-regression-with-agroclimatic-metrics.html
23 Chapter 24 - Examples of PLS regression with agroclimatic metrics 23.1 Questions 23.2 Answers

23 Chapter 24 - Examples of PLS regression with agroclimatic metrics 23.1 Questions Look across all the PLS results presented above. Can you detect a pattern in where chilling and forcing periods could be delineated clearly, and where this attempt failed? Think about possible reasons for the success or failure of PLS analysis based on agroclimatic metrics. Write down your thoughts 23.2 Answers When are chilling and forcing phases clear? Forcing (spring heat): usually easy to detect. Rising spring temperatures vary between years and directly shift flowering/budburst → clear time window in the model. Chilling (winter cold): depends on climate. Cold regions: winters almost always meet the chill requirement → little year‑to‑year variation → statistically hard to see, even if biologically important. Mild/warm winters: chill is sometimes limiting → strong variation → chilling window becomes clear. Very warm sites: both chilling and forcing can be visible. Dominance case: if flowering mainly depends on chill (e.g., very low‑chill environments), the forcing signal may look weak. Summary: Cold winters → chilling hidden; warm winters → chilling visible; strong spring warming → forcing visible. Why does PLS sometimes work well and sometimes not? Low variability/saturation: if chill is always fulfilled, it cannot explain year differences → model assigns variation to forcing. Correlated temperatures: warm winters often lead to early springs → chilling and forcing signals overlap → fuzzy time windows. Species/cultivar biology: some plants are chill‑driven, others heat‑driven → one process can mask the other. Gradual dormancy: real transitions are continuous, but PLS searches for discrete periods → boundaries are approximate. Metric choice & data quality: wrong chill model, short time series, or poor site representation weaken results. Conclusion: PLS is a good exploratory tool to locate sensitive phenological periods, but results should be interpreted as likely windows, not exact physiological mechanisms. 25 Chapter 26 - Evaluating PLS outputs This chapter is more about interpretation, rather than doing something new in R. I used the Boskoop dataset and the weatherdata of Klein Altendorf since it wasnt specified, that we should use the data of our location we choose before, I worked with the patched data for Campus Klein-Altendorf. In the end I used the very same script as the script used in chapter 26 combined with the PLS function from Chapter 23. I also did some adjustments to the rendering of the plot, because some parts turned out very small, when I rendered them in a higher resolution. 25.1 Questions Reproduce the analysis for the ‘Roter Boskoop’ dataset. We’ve looked at data from a number of locations so far. How would you expect this surface plot to look like in Beijing? And how should it look in Tunisia? 25.2 Answers Klein Altendorf, Germany Looking at the plot, we can see that we have rather strange VIP values for the chilling. There is not really a big coherent section for chill. When it comes to heat VIP, you can see it better. From January on until the end of April we have a section with a lot og heat. The same pattern can be seen in the Model coefficients (MC). For chill we dont have a big section in red, but we have very little indicators for a delaying bloom. For heat it looks different. The heat, and the aspect of “advancing”, increases from beginning of January until the begging of April. Therefore the conclusion is: Chill accumulation occurs mainly in winter, whereas heat accumulation begins in early spring, supporting the identification of chilling and forcing phases. Beijing, China Beijing has very cold winters, so the chilling requirement of temperate fruit trees will almost always be fulfilled. Because dormancy release is reliably achieved, flowering no longer depends strongly on winter temperature. Instead, bloom timing mainly depends on how warm the spring is and how fast heat accumulates. In the surface plot this means winter temperature has only a weak effect, while spring temperature has a strong effect. The contour lines would therefore appear almost vertical. Different winter temperatures would lead to similar bloom dates, but small differences in spring temperature would shift flowering noticeably. Therefore, in Beijing the system is mainly controlled by the forcing phase. Sfax, Tunisia Tunisia has mild winters, and the chilling requirement is often not completely fulfilled. In this case trees remain partly dormant and cannot respond properly to spring warming until enough chilling has accumulated. As a result, winter temperature becomes the main factor determining bloom timing. In the surface plot, bloom date would vary strongly with winter temperature but only weakly with spring temperature. The contour lines would therefore appear almost horizontal. Even a warm spring could not fully compensate for insufficient winter chilling. Thus, in Tunisia flowering is mainly limited by the chilling phase. 25.3 Code library(chillR) library(fields) library(reshape2) library(metR) library(ggplot2) library(colorRamps) library(tidyverse) Bosk_first <- read_tab("data/Roter_Boskoop_bloom_1958_2019.csv") %>% select(Pheno_year, First_bloom) %>% mutate(Year = as.numeric(substr(First_bloom, 1, 4)), Month = as.numeric(substr(First_bloom, 5, 6)), Day = as.numeric(substr(First_bloom, 7, 8))) %>% make_JDay() %>% select(Pheno_year, JDay) %>% rename(Year = Pheno_year, pheno = JDay) temps <- read_tab("data/Beijing_weather.csv") temps_hourly <- temps %>% stack_hourly_temps(latitude = 34.4) daychill <- daily_chill(hourtemps = temps_hourly, running_mean = 1, models = list(Chilling_Hours = Chilling_Hours, Utah_Chill_Units = Utah_Model, Chill_Portions = Dynamic_Model, GDH = GDH) ) plscf <- PLS_chill_force(daily_chill_obj = daychill, bio_data_frame = Bosk_first, split_month = 6, chill_models = "Chill_Portions", heat_models = "GDH", runn_means = 11) # chapter-27---the-relative-importance-of-chill-and-heat.html
26 Chapter 27 - The relative importance of chill and heat 26.1 Question 26.2 Answer

26 Chapter 27 - The relative importance of chill and heat 26.1 Question Describe the temperature response hypothesis outlined in this chapter. 26.2 Answer The chapter proposes that the relative importance of chilling and forcing temperatures for spring phenology depends on the winter climate. Figure 26.1: Hypothetical response of temperate tree phenology to temperature during the chilling and forcing periods: (Guo et al. 2015). In cold climates, trees usually receive sufficient winter chill. Therefore, variation in chilling temperatures has little effect on bloom timing. Instead, phenology mainly responds to spring heat accumulation (forcing), and warmer spring temperatures lead to earlier budbreak. As winter temperatures become warmer, chilling becomes less effective. Under these conditions, higher temperatures during the chilling period can delay dormancy release. In this intermediate climate range, both chilling and forcing influence bloom timing. In very warm climates, chilling becomes the dominant limiting factor. Insufficient chill delays budbreak, even if spring temperatures are high. As a result, continued warming may slow down phenological advances or even cause later flowering. Overall, the hypothesis predicts a shift along a climate gradient: from heat-controlled phenology in cold regions to chill-controlled phenology in warm regions. References Guo, Liang, Junhu Dai, Mingcheng Wang, Jianchu Xu, and Eike Luedeling. 2015. “Responses of Spring Phenology in Temperate Zone Trees to Climate Warming: A Case Study of Apricot Flowering in China.” Agricultural and Forest Meteorology 201: 1–7. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agrformet.2014.10.016.
chapter-29---making-valid-tree-phenology-models.html
27 Chapter 29 - Making valid tree phenology models 27.1 Questions 27.2 Answers

27 Chapter 29 - Making valid tree phenology models 27.1 Questions Explain the difference between output validation and process validation. Explain what a validity domain is and why it is important to consider this whenever we want to use our model to forecast something. What is validation for purpose? How can we ensure that our model is suitable for the predictions we want to make? 27.2 Answers 1. Difference between output validation and process validation Output validation evaluates whether a model can reproduce observed data, for example whether predicted bloom dates match measured bloom dates. However, a model can produce correct results for the wrong reasons. Process validation instead examines whether the internal mechanisms of the model represent the real biological processes. It compares the processes included in the model with current biological knowledge about dormancy, chilling and forcing. Therefore, process validation provides stronger evidence that the model is scientifically meaningful. 2. What is a validity domain and why is it important? The validity domain is the range of environmental conditions for which a model can make reliable predictions. It is defined by the conditions under which the model was calibrated and tested. Predictions outside this range are uncertain because the system may behave differently. This is especially important for climate-change studies, since future temperatures may exceed the range of past observations. Using a model outside its validity domain can lead to misleading forecasts. This problem can also be visualized. It was done in the module by using the following plot: Figure 27.1: Example of limited validity domain: Comparison of historical temperature conditions (‘Past combined’) with projected future climate scenarios in Tmin–Tmax space. The black polygons represent the temperature range used to calibrate the phenology model. Colored areas extending beyond this range indicate conditions outside the model’s validity domain. The marked red circle illustrates the data which is ‘outside’ of the ‘Past combined’ data and can be uncertain. 3. What is validation for purpose? Validation for purpose means evaluating a model based on the specific question it is intended to answer. A model may be suitable for explaining past phenology but not for predicting future climate responses. Therefore, we must first define the intended application of the model and then assess whether the model is capable of performing this task reliably. 4. How can we ensure the model is suitable for the predictions we want to make? To ensure suitability, the model should be tested under conditions similar to those for which predictions are required. This includes validating the model across the full range of expected temperatures and incorporating biological mechanisms such as chilling and heat accumulation. Combining historical observations with experimental data that extend the temperature range can help cover future climate conditions. Only if the model performs well across this range can we trust its projections.

28 Chapter 30 - The PhenoFlex model 28.1 Questions 28.2 Answers 28.3 Code

28 Chapter 30 - The PhenoFlex model In this chapter I worked with the PhenoFlex phenology model, which combines chilling accumulation and heat accumulation to predict bloom dates of temperate fruit trees. The goal was to parameterize the model for the cultivar Roter Boskoop, generate predictions of bloom dates, and evaluate model performance using different error metrics. The chapter helped me understand how phenology models can integrate biological processes such as dormancy release and heat accumulation. One of the main challenges for me was understanding the large number of parameters in the model and how they influence the predictions. Parameterization required fitting the model to observed bloom data and interpreting the solver results. Another challenge was interpreting the model evaluation metrics (RMSEP, mean error and mean absolute error) and assessing how well the model reproduces observed bloom dates. Overall, the chapter helped me understand how phenology models can be calibrated and evaluated using real temperature and phenology data. 28.1 Questions Parameterize the PhenoFlex model for `Roter Boskoop’ apples. Produce plots of predicted vs. observed bloom dates and distribution of prediction errors. Compute the model performance metrics RMSEP, mean error and mean absolute error. 28.2 Answers Parameters for “Roter Boskoop” dataset Phenoflparam <- read.csv("data/PhenoFlex_parameters_Roter_Boskoop.csv") X x 1 3.945790e+01 2 1.955518e+02 3 3.217589e-01 4 2.500000e+01 5 3.372800e+03 6 9.900300e+03 7 6.187399e+03 8 5.939931e+13 9 4.978583e+00 10 3.006917e+01 11 4.000000e+00 12 2.494781e+00 Plots Figure 28.1: The histogram shows the distribution of differences between predicted and observed bloom dates. Most prediction errors are centered around zero, indicating that the model reproduces observed bloom dates with relatively small deviations. Figure 28.2: Each point represents one season. The diagonal line indicates perfect agreement between predicted and observed bloom dates. The close clustering of points around the line suggests that the PhenoFlex model predicts bloom timing with good accuracy. Statistical Errors RMSEP: The RMSEP (Root Mean Square Error of Prediction) describes the overall prediction accuracy of the model. In this analysis the RMSEP was 4.69, which is lower than the value reported in the chapter example (about 6.1). This indicates that the PhenoFlex model reproduces the observed bloom dates of Roter Boskoop apples with a high level of accuracy. ## [1] 4.690369 Mean Error: The mean error describes the average bias of the model predictions. In this case the mean error was −1.92, meaning that the model predicts bloom dates on average about two days earlier than the observed dates. This bias is relatively small and indicates that the model predictions are generally well balanced. ## [1] -1.915972 Mean Absolute Error: The mean absolute error represents the average absolute deviation between predicted and observed bloom dates. In this analysis the value was 3.34 days, which is lower than the value reported in the chapter example (4.62 days). This suggests that the model predictions are typically only a few days away from the observed bloom dates, indicating good model performance. ## [1] 3.340972 28.3 Code library(chillR) library(tidyverse) CKA_weather <- read_tab("data/TMaxTMin1958-2019_patched.csv") hourtemps <- stack_hourly_temps(CKA_weather, latitude = 50.6) yc <- 40 zc <- 190 iSeason <- genSeason(hourtemps, mrange = c(8, 6), years = c(2009)) season_data <- hourtemps$hourtemps[iSeason[[1]],] res <- PhenoFlex(temp = season_data$Temp, times = c(1: length(season_data$Temp)), zc = zc, stopatzc = TRUE, yc = yc, basic_output = FALSE) # Plot DBreakDay <- res$bloomindex seasontemps <- hourtemps$hourtemps[iSeason[[1]],] seasontemps[,"x"] <- res$x seasontemps[,"y"] <- res$y seasontemps[,"z"] <- res$z seasontemps <- add_date(seasontemps) CR_full <- seasontemps$Date[which(seasontemps$y >= yc)[1]] Bloom <- seasontemps$Date[which(seasontemps$z >= zc)[1]] chillplot <- ggplot(data = seasontemps[1:DBreakDay,], aes(x = Date, y = y)) + geom_line(col = "blue", lwd = 1.5) + theme_bw(base_size = 20) + geom_hline(yintercept = yc, lty = 2, col = "blue", lwd = 1.2) + geom_vline(xintercept = CR_full, lty = 3, col = "blue", lwd = 1.2) + ylab("Chill accumulation (y)") + labs(title = "Chilling") + annotate("text", label = "Chill req. chapter-32---can-we-improve-the-performance-of-phenoflex.html
30 Chapter 32 - Can we improve the performance of PhenoFlex? 30.1 Questions 30.2 Answers

30 Chapter 32 - Can we improve the performance of PhenoFlex? 30.1 Questions What was the objective of this work? What was the main conclusion? What experiments could we conduct to test the hypothesis that emerged at the end of the conclusion? 30.2 Answers 1. What was the objective of this work? The objective of this work was to evaluate whether the performance of the PhenoFlex phenology model can be improved by modifying the calibration dataset. In particular, the study investigated the effect of including or excluding marginal winter seasons, which are seasons with unusually warm winter temperatures that may not represent typical dormancy conditions. The goal was to determine whether these atypical seasons influence the estimation of model parameters and thereby affect the predictive accuracy of the model. The model can look something like this: Figure 30.1: Chill and heat response plots for two versions of the PhenoFlex model: (Luedeling et al. 2021). 2. What was the main conclusion? The main conclusion was that including marginal seasons with unusually warm winter conditions reduced the performance of the PhenoFlex model. When these seasons were excluded, the model predicted bloom dates more accurately. This suggests that the model does not fully represent biological processes occurring under extreme temperature conditions, especially during dormancy release. 3. What experiments could be conducted to test the emerging hypothesis? To test the hypothesis, controlled experiments should expose trees to a wide range of winter temperature conditions. For example, tree-moving or climate-chamber experiments could simulate warmer and colder winters and measure dormancy release and bloom timing. Such systematic experiments would help determine whether additional biological mechanisms influence dormancy breaking that are not currently included in the model. References Luedeling, Eike, Katja Schiffers, Till Fohrmann, and Carsten Urbach. 2021. “PhenoFlex - an Integrated Model to Predict Spring Phenology in Temperate Fruit Trees.” Agricultural and Forest Meteorology 307: 108491. https://doi.org/https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agrformet.2021.108491.
